does: This paper evaluates prompt-injection attacks against current frontier computer-using agents (Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.4) by reproducing hand-crafted attack templates from recent red-teaming literature. The authors release CUA-HandCrafted, a benchmark comprising 793 episodes across 24 multi-step web tasks, 56 attack templates spanning 8 families, and 4 system-prompt configurations. Against browser-based tasks, they measure 0/140 multi-step attack successes (upper bound 2.60%), with ablations showing this resistance stems from model weights rather than inference-time defenses. However, the same model weights remain vulnerable to skill-injection attacks on a coding-agent benchmark (SkillBench), achieving up to 100% attack success rate. The core finding is that frontier safety hardening is domain-conditioned and specifically targets the browser interaction surface, while remaining vulnerable in other domains.
connects: This work directly engages with recent computer-using-agent red-teaming literature reporting 42-98% attack success rates, investigating whether those high figures reflect the attack methodology itself or rely on specific model properties and domain characteristics. It builds on prior work examining prompt-injection vulnerabilities in language models, adversarial attacks against autonomous agents, and safety evaluation benchmarks for AI systems. The paper extends existing evaluation frameworks by providing systematic, reproducible assessment of hand-crafted attack templates across multiple domains rather than cherry-picking vulnerable models. It connects to broader literature on domain-specific safety properties, generalization of adversarial robustness across capabilities, and the role of reinforcement learning in shaping model vulnerabilities, while responding to methodological concerns about headline attack success rates in published red-teaming studies.
enables: This work opens directions for understanding how safety properties generalize and fail to generalize across different interaction modalities and domains. It enables future research into domain-conditioned safety mechanisms, investigating whether current hardening approaches are brittle surface-level patches or reflect deeper robustness. The benchmark and findings motivate development of cross-domain adversarial evaluation frameworks that test whether safety improvements in one domain transfer to others. The observation that RL-optimized injection text rather than attack categories drives high success rates suggests research into how optimization processes shape vulnerability landscapes differently across domains. Future work can build on this reproducibility audit to establish more rigorous standards for red-teaming methodology, develop domain-agnostic safety approaches, and create comprehensive multi-domain benchmarks for evaluating computer-using agent robustness.
